content.subtitle: Position of Adjectives
items: After verbs such as appear, be, become, feel, get, grow, look, seem, smell, sound, taste and turn
examples: She wore a **magnificent** gown to the ceremony.
By the end of the hike, my feet felt **sore**.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: The verbs in the table above are not normally followed by adverbs. However, some of them can be followed by adverbs when the verb refers to an action.
After as, how, so, this (= so), that (= so) and too, adjectives come before the article.
Some adjectives only appear after a verb and not before a noun. These include adjectives beginning with a-, such as afraid, aghast, alike, alive, alone, asleep, awake, etc.
examples: ✓ The security guard **looked suspiciously** at everyone who entered the building.
✓ I'd never want to work in as **demanding a role** as hers.
✓ It's **so/that demanding a role** that most people turn it down.
✓ How **rewarding a career** must medicine be!
✓ This is **too heavy a parcel** for me to carry on my own.
✓ A child was **asleep** in the back of the car.
✗ There was an **asleep** child in the back of the car.

content.subtitle: Order of Adjectives
content.description: When more than one adjective is used before a noun, they usually appear in the following order, sometimes separated by commas: judgement, size, shape, colour, origin, material, purpose
examples: They rented a **charming little stone cottage** by the sea.
She admired his **thick, dark, Italian, leather** jacket.
What the kitchen really needs is a **large pine dining table**.

content.subtitle: Comparative
content.description: to compare things or people that are different
examples: Their new apartment is much **larger** and **more modern** than ours.

content.subtitle: Superlative
content.description: to compare one member of a group of people or things with the whole group
examples: Cape Town is probably my **least favourite** city to drive in. I still think my home town is the **best place** I've ever lived.

content.subtitle: Comparative and Superlative Modifiers
items: Modifiers with comparatives: (quite) a bit, a great deal, a good deal, a little, (quite) a lot, any, considerably, even, far, just, little, much, no, slightly, somewhat
Modifiers with superlatives: by far, far and away, easily, far from, much, quite
examples: Since the new road opened, the town centre has become **considerably** more accessible and far less congested.
Most locals would agree that Riverdale is **easily** the most peaceful suburb to live in.

content.subtitle: Structures used to make comparisons
items: nothing like as ... as / nowhere near as ... as
examples: Silver is roughly **half** as expensive as gold.
Cotton isn't **nearly** as durable as nylon.
Cotton is **nothing like** as / nowhere near as durable as nylon.
The **longer** the delay, **the more frustrated** passengers become.
✓ Steel is not nearly as flexible as rubber. (large difference between the things being referred to)
✓ Today's session was not quite as productive as yesterday's. (small difference between the things being referred to)

content.subtitle: The + adjective to refer to a group of people
content.description: We can use **the** + adjective (without a noun) to refer to a group of people sharing a common characteristic. The verb is always plural. We never add **-s** to the adjective.
items: To refer to a general social group (defined by an ongoing characteristic)
To refer to a specific group (defined by a situation or context)
examples: Governments must do far more to support **the homeless**.
Tax reforms often benefit **the wealthy** at the expense of **the poor**.
After the earthquake, **the displaced** were given emergency shelter.
Field hospitals were set up to treat **the injured**.

content.subtitle: The + adjective for nationalities
content.description: We use **the** + adjective to refer to the people of some nations, particularly those whose adjective ends in **-ch**, **-sh**, **-ese** or **-ss**. The verb is always plural.
examples: **The Dutch** are internationally recognised for their innovative approach to water management.
**The French** are passionate about their cuisine.
**The Japanese** are renowned for their precision engineering.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Not all nationality adjectives follow this pattern. For many nationalities we simply use the plural noun form instead of **the** + adjective.
We say **the Germans / the Italians / the Brazilians**, NOT ~~the German / the Italian / the Brazilian~~ (when referring to the whole nation).
examples: ✓ **The Germans** are known for their engineering.
✗ **The German** are known for their engineering.
✓ **The French** are known for their cuisine.
✗ **The Frances** are known for their cuisine.

content.subtitle: General Rules
content.description: There are three places in a clause where an adverb (or adverbial phrase) might appear: at the beginning, at the end and with the verb. Different kinds of adverb go in different positions, and some may go in more than one position.
examples: Adverbs do not normally appear between a verb and its direct object: ✗ She **explained very clearly** the problem. ✓ She explained the problem **very clearly**.
With verbs formed using auxiliary verbs, the adverb normally follows the first auxiliary: The bridge **has always attracted** photographers and tourists.
Adverbs of frequency (always, often, etc) follow auxiliary verbs and be and come before other verbs: He's **rarely** in his office on Fridays. He **rarely goes** to his office on Fridays.
Connecting adverbs usually go at the beginning of a clause: They invested all their savings in the property; **then**, the market collapsed.

content.subtitle: Definitions
items: **Ungradable** adjectives describe qualities which are extreme and which cannot be 'more' or 'less', eg amazing, dead, exhausted, fantastic, helpless, impossible, incredible, necessary, perfect, pointless, right, splendid, unacceptable, wonderful, wrong, etc.

content.subtitle: Modifiers
items: Modifiers with ungradable adjectives: absolutely, completely, quite, totally, utterly, etc
Modifiers with gradable adjectives: a bit, a little, fairly, quite, really, too, very, etc
examples: After running her third marathon of the year, Nina was **absolutely exhausted**.
Dan was **a bit tired** after the run, but he recovered quickly.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: When **quite** is used with gradable adjectives, it means 'rather, fairly'. When **quite** is used with ungradable adjectives, it means 'absolutely, completely'.
examples: ✓ The studio flat was **quite** nice, but a little cramped for two people.
✗ The view from the penthouse was **quite** magnificent! (= absolutely)

content.subtitle: Words with the same form as adjective and adverb
examples: ✓ The icy roads made driving **hard**.
✓ There was **hardly** any traffic on the motorway this morning.
✓ She trained **hard** every day in preparation for the competition.
✗ She trained too **hardly** and ended up injuring her knee.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: Some adjectives end in –ly, eg costly, deadly, friendly, likely, lively, lonely, lovely. They do not form adverbs, but we often use a phrase such as in a … way to describe how something is done.
examples: ✓ The staff always greet customers **in a very friendly way**.
